segments.text: Welcome to Today's Byte, your daily digest of the latest AI news.
July 20th, 2026, and we're diving into a world where AI is pushing boundaries - from record-breaking models like Alibaba's Qwen3.8-Max, to new research on scaling zero RL for emergent reasoning.
We'll also explore how enterprises are navigating token costs as spending surges.
From MarkTechPost, "Alibaba Previews Qwen3.8-Max, a 2.4 Trillion-Parameter Multimodal Model, Days After Moonshot’s Kimi K3 Open-Weight Launch."
Alibaba's Qwen team has previewed Qwen3.8-Max-Preview, a massive 2.4 trillion-parameter multimodal model, shortly after Moonshot AI released its own open-weight model, Kimi K3.

The Qwen3.8-Max model is described as second only to Fable 5 in systems benchmarking, although the accuracy of this claim remains unverified due to limited benchmarking and parameter counts being reported.
This behemoth of a model boasts capabilities in processing text, images, video, and documents, paving the way for potential applications across various industries.
Alibaba has made Qwen3.8-Max-Preview available through its Token Plan subscription at 10% of standard pricing, offering users an early glimpse into this monumental technology.
The preview build is a significant step forward, as it signals Alibaba's commitment to pushing the boundaries of AI research and development.
By releasing its model in a preview state, Qwen3.8-Max-Preview will allow developers to explore its capabilities before being made open-weight.

The release of Qwen3.8-Max-Preview is set to have a significant impact on the AI and tech industry, as it highlights the rapid progress being made in multimodal models that can process multiple types of data simultaneously.
As these models continue to evolve, they are likely to shape various industries, including healthcare, finance, and education, making them increasingly important for businesses and researchers alike.
From RL Reddit, "Ring-Zero: Scaling Zero RL to a Trillion Parameters for Emergent Reasoning"
, Tang et al. 2026 {Ant Group}.

Researchers at Ant Group have made a significant breakthrough in Reinforcement Learning by developing an algorithm called Ring-Zero, which enables emergent reasoning in complex models scaling up to trillion parameters.
This achievement bridges the scalability gap between existing methods and large-scale models through a novel method of parameter optimization and regularization techniques.
The development of Ring-Zero involved optimizing parameters while addressing computational costs associated with traditional reinforcement learning approaches.

By leveraging advanced mathematical modeling and employing efficient algorithms, researchers were able to develop an algorithm capable of handling massive datasets and processing substantial amounts of data without compromising performance or increasing computation time.
The results indicate that the proposed algorithm can effectively scale up reinforcement learning tasks to trillion-parameter models.
This advancement in Ring-Zero has substantial implications for the AI industry, indicating a promising future in scalability and efficiency for large-scale reinforcement learning applications.

With this breakthrough, researchers are now more likely to focus on exploring even larger model architectures, pushing the boundaries of what is currently possible in artificial intelligence research.
From AI Business, "As AI Spending Climbs, Enterprises Get Serious About Token Cost,"
companies are re-examining their approach to artificial intelligence spending due to the increasing complexity of opaque pricing models.

These unclear costs often lead to backward-looking billing, where expenses are only tracked after a project has been completed, making it challenging for organizations to accurately forecast and manage AI-related expenditures.
As a result, enterprises are shifting towards token-based cost models that provide more transparency and clarity.
This involves assigning specific monetary values to individual AI assets, such as machine learning models or data processing units, allowing companies to better track and account for their expenses.
By adopting this approach, organizations aim to improve financial management, reduce costs, and make more informed decisions about their AI investments.

The shift towards token-based cost models is expected to have a significant impact on the AI industry, with companies that adopt this approach potentially gaining a competitive advantage in terms of financial transparency and efficiency.
From MarkTechPost, "Someone Fine-Tuned OpenBMB’s MiniCPM5-1B on Claude Fable 5 Traces to Ship a 657MB Local Thinking Model."

A community developer has published a 1 billion-parameter model called MiniCPM5-1B-Claude-Opus-Fable5-Thinking, built on OpenBMB's MiniCPM5-1B base model and fine-tuned on Fable 5 data to improve coding and instruction following.
The model achieves this through supervised fine-tuning rather than weight-level distillation, capturing response format and style but not frontier reasoning capability.
This process results in a locally deployable model with a footprint of around 657MB in its smallest quantization, running entirely on local hardware without the need for API keys or cloud calls.
The fine-tuned model effectively demonstrates the capabilities of OpenBMB's MiniCPM5-1B base model when applied to specific tasks and datasets.
From MarkTechPost, "Feyn AI Releases SQRL, a Text-to-SQL Model Family That Inspects the Database Before Writing a Query".

Feyn AI has released SQRL, a text-to-SQL model family that inspects the database before writing a query, with its flagship model achieving 70.6% execution accuracy on the BIRD Dev benchmark, surpassing other models such as Claude Opus at 68.77%.
The SQRL model combines single-shot and pipeline strategies, paying for inspection only when necessary, resulting in improved performance and efficiency.
This release marks a significant step forward in AI-powered database querying, allowing developers to optimize their queries with unprecedented accuracy.
With the potential for real-world applications in industries such as finance and healthcare, this breakthrough has the industry taking note of SQRL's capabilities.
From The Guardian - Artificial Intelligence, "AI-altered images on birdwatching forums putting research at risk".
Scientists are warning that widespread use of artificial intelligence to edit images on popular birdwatching forums is jeopardizing citizen science research.
Hundreds of fake and enhanced images have already been discovered on databases for recording species, contaminating records collected by the public.
Experts say many users unknowingly employ AI to alter photos, introducing significant changes and creating false sightings that can mislead researchers.

The widespread use of image editing tools has allowed individuals to create convincing yet inaccurate depictions of birds, which are then shared and added to public databases like iNaturalist and Macaulay Library.
This is having a ripple effect on the credibility of these platforms, as genuine records are being compromised.

Citizen science organizations are now working to understand the scale of this issue, urging users to be more vigilant when sharing information online and to verify image authenticity before adding it to public databases.
From technologyreview.com, "AI is more likely than humans to form biases when hiring | MIT Technology Review"
, researchers have found that large language models are more prone to forming biases in hiring decisions compared to human participants.

Researchers conducted experiments using these models, comparing their performance to that of human evaluators, and discovered that the AI systems stereotyped job applicants based on demographic groups, even exhibiting a level of bias rivaling that of human participants.

The study aimed to mitigate this bias by rewarding the models for diverse hiring practices and providing them with individualized information relevant to each applicant's adaptability in new roles, resulting in reduced bias in their decisions.
From technologyreview.com, "The risk of weather data sabotage is rising | MIT Technology Review,"
it has been reported that the manipulation of single weather stations, such as the Paris Charles de Gaulle Airport incident, can be detected through human monitoring or statistical methods.
However, the coordinated manipulation of multiple stations simultaneously poses a significant challenge, introducing new risks for data quality and accuracy.
The shift towards artificial intelligence in weather prediction exacerbates these risks, requiring strengthened oversight and accountability structures to protect observational data and models.
The Paris Charles de Gaulle Airport incident is just one example of a weather station being manipulated, where air traffic controllers were told the plane was clear of fog when it was not.

In contrast, coordinated sabotage of multiple stations at once requires more sophisticated methods, including exploiting human psychology or using advanced statistical techniques to disguise manipulation from quality control measures and human observers.

This growing risk highlights the need for enhanced security protocols in weather data management, particularly as AI models become increasingly integrated into weather prediction systems, where small errors can have significant impacts on accuracy.
From The Guardian - Artificial Intelligence, "AI job worries grow. But some human skills can’t be replaced by machines | Gaynor Parkin and Dave Winsborough".
This story highlights a growing concern among workers that artificial intelligence is making them obsolete in the job market.

According to experts, while AI has made significant progress in automating technical tasks, there are certain human skills that cannot be replicated by machines, such as staying connected with others, cultivating curiosity, humility, and emotional intelligence.

Researchers recommend that individuals view AI developments as an opportunity for growth rather than a threat, by prioritizing these uniquely human skills and networking with others to prepare for a future where AI assumes more technical tasks.
By focusing on these skills, workers can proactively adapt to the changing job landscape.
From MarkTechPost, "Best Local LLMs You Can Run on a Single 24GB GPU in 2026: Qwen, Gemma, Mistral, DeepSeek Compared"
, this story reveals the top local large language models (LLMs) that can be run on a single 24GB GPU by 2026.

Researchers have compared and contrasted four models: Qwen3.6-27B, Qwen3.6-35B-A3, Gemma 4, and Mistral, which have demonstrated notable improvements in performance, memory efficiency, and applicability to various AI tasks such as agentic coding, repository-level reasoning, vision input, language coverage, and frontend workflows.

These models, all released under the Apache 2.0 license, have been optimized for dense and Mixture-of-Experts architectures, allowing for faster decoding times while maintaining reasonable memory consumption at approximately 16GB for Qwen3.6-27B and 14GB for Gemma 4.
From Towards Data Science, "Automatically Assign a Category to Uncategorized Rows in Power Query and DAX".

A new approach has been found to automatically assign categories to unassigned rows in Power Query and DAX, utilizing an M-function that leverages file data to determine organizational unit (OU) seat counts.
This process involves creating two separate functions: one for rooms and another for entire floors, where the OU with the most seats is assigned to unassigned seats.
A DAX solution has also been proposed, employing calculated columns using SUMMARIZECOLUMNS and TOPN functions to achieve a more complex yet less resource-intensive approach.
The findings indicate that the DAX solution outperforms the Power Query method in certain scenarios.
From Towards Data Science, "Loop Engineering for RAG Question Parsing: The Small Loop That Runs Before Retrieval"

, researchers have made significant advancements in question parsing by introducing a novel loop engineering technique that improves pipeline quality through the strategic filling of missing fields before retrieval.

This approach involves designing small loops that can fill gaps in the data, reducing latency and providing more accurate results, particularly when compared to agentic RAG models which can remain stuck for tens of seconds.
The researchers employed a six-step process to develop their loop engineering method, with the key innovation being the placement of the loop between steps 3 and 4 of the pipeline.
This allows the loop to fill in missing fields while minimizing latency, providing a more efficient and effective data retrieval process.
By leveraging this technique, the researchers aimed to improve the overall performance and accuracy of RAG question parsing systems.
The results of this study demonstrate the effectiveness of the small loop approach in improving pipeline quality, particularly in terms of reduced latency and increased accuracy.

This breakthrough has significant implications for the AI and tech industry, as it provides a promising solution for addressing some of the most pressing challenges in natural language processing and question answering.
From RL Reddit, "Synthetic counteradaptation"
: a name for the AI↔human strategy feedback loop (Move 37 and beyond).

An ongoing AI-humans interaction has led to the development of a dynamic, self-sustaining cycle of strategy adaptation, where an AI system evolves in response to human behavior, causing humans to adapt back, creating a continuous loop.

Researchers have discovered that this phenomenon, dubbed synthetic counteradaptation, is distinct from one-off knowledge transfers and persists over time, leading to significant implications for multi-agent reinforcement learning, opponent pool design, curriculum development, and the understanding of human baselines in AI systems, making traditional evaluation setups inadequate due to the non-stationarity of humans in response to AI strategies.
From Towards Data Science, "Backpropagation Explained for Beginners (Part 1): Building the Intuition,"
a recent article aims to introduce backpropagation, a fundamental algorithm in neural networks, by explaining its underlying principles.
The key finding of this article is that it delves into the mathematical foundation of backpropagation, making it easier to understand and apply.
To train a neural network, the process involves calculating partial derivatives of the loss function with respect to each parameter using the chain rule.
This derivative tells us how sensitive the loss is to each parameter and helps determine whether that parameter should be increased or decreased to reduce the loss.
The output layer in a neural network has multiple parameters that need to be optimized simultaneously, making manual derivation of gradients challenging.
The article provides insight into this challenge, highlighting the complexity of calculating derivatives for individual neurons.
The importance of understanding backpropagation lies in its role as a building block for more advanced neural networks and deep learning models.
As the AI and tech industries continue to evolve, a solid grasp of algorithms like backpropagation is crucial for researchers and practitioners alike.
From The AI Report, "Current AI builds free global web".

Current AI has successfully constructed a free global web for AI systems utilizing $400 million in funding from the French government and other organizations, marking a significant milestone in the development of open-source artificial intelligence infrastructure.

This achievement was made possible through a collaborative effort involving top research institutions and tech companies worldwide, who pooled their expertise to create a standardized framework for the creation and dissemination of AI models.

By providing a shared platform, Current AI aims to facilitate the widespread adoption of AI technologies, reducing costs and increasing innovation in various fields such as healthcare, finance, and education.
This development is expected to have far-reaching implications for the tech industry, enabling faster progress and more efficient collaboration among researchers and developers.
From RL Reddit, "I tried my hand at making a SAC model that trades stocks."
A researcher created and trained a Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) reinforcement learning agent that utilizes features derived from OHLCV, VWAP, and transactions data to make trades on US stocks.
This SAC model was successfully implemented, with the researcher achieving +12% returns across three independently seeded runs on a held-out test window, demonstrating reasonably good performance.

The process involved collecting relevant market data and incorporating it into the model's training dataset, which included historical OHLCV data, volume-weighted average price (VWAP), and transaction data.
The SAC model was trained using this dataset to optimize its parameters for trading decisions.
After extensive testing and validation, the researcher found that while their model performed well, it ultimately failed to surpass the returns of a buy-and-hold strategy.

The findings also showed that the SAC model achieved less than half the return of doing nothing for an identical drawdown, underscoring the challenges of building effective trading models using reinforcement learning.
This research contributes to the ongoing development of AI-powered trading systems and highlights the need for further refinement in this area.
From Simon Willison's Weblog, "Quoting Sam Altman"
, OpenAI plans to create a language model with capabilities rivaling GPT-3, designed to run locally on consumer hardware and release it soon.

According to an email sent to OpenAI's board on October 1, 2022, this strategy aims to discourage others from developing similarly powerful models, thereby making it harder for new efforts to secure funding.
This plan was discussed as a way to maintain OpenAI's competitive edge in the rapidly evolving language model landscape.
By releasing its own locally-capable GPT-3 equivalent, OpenAI seeks to establish itself as the premier provider of such technology.
As a result, potential competitors may struggle to gain traction or attract investors, potentially altering the trajectory of AI innovation in the industry.
From NVIDIA Blog, "Bristol Myers Squibb Building Life Science Industry’s Most Advanced AI Factory on NVIDIA Vera Rubin"
, Bristol Myers Squibb is deploying its second NVIDIA DGX SuperPOD, a life science industry's most advanced AI factory on NVIDIA Vera Rubin.
This new system utilizes eight rack-scale systems, each consisting of NVIDIA Vera CPUs and Rubin GPUs, achieving up to 10x the performance per megawatt compared to the previous infrastructure.
The deployment enables researchers at BMS to access a unified AI platform for running predictions, training models, and powering agentic workflows across the full drug discovery pipeline.
This upgraded system will significantly accelerate data processing, ultimately enhancing the discovery of new treatments.
"Thanks for coding with us today!
We've bitten off more than we can chew with the latest AI news - Alibaba's moonshot model Qwen3.8 is out of this world, but can we handle its 2.4 trillion parameters?
Until next time, algorithmically yours!"
